EF1 TORNADO WITH 90 MPH WINDS. LIKELY STARTED IN ADAMS COUNTY AND CROSSED INTO MARQUETTE COUNTY 2.5 NORTH OF BRIGGSVILLE. TORNADO CONTINUED EAST-SOUTHEAST 2.5 MILES AND ENDED 2 MILES NORTHEAST OF BRIGGSVILLE NEAR WHERE HIGHWAY 23 CROSSES NEENAH CREEK. TREES DOWN...DAMAGE TO BARNS...HOMES TOTALED ABOUT 300,000 DOLLARS.
A FEW TREES AND TREE BRANCHES REPORTED DOWN IN THE SOUTHWEST PART OF THE COUNTY NEAR BRIGGSVILLE AND ENDEAVOR. POWER LINES ALSO REPORTED DOWN.
CALLS STILL COMING IN TO POLICE DEPARTMENT...BUT TREES WERE BLOWN DOWN AND SOME FLOODING WAS ALSO REPORTED. THE DEGREE OF FLOODING IS UNKNOWN.

TEMPORARY CANOPY BLOWN DOWN...WIND SPEED UNKNOWN
TREES COMING DOWN AND ESTIMATED 60 MPH WINDS ALL ALONG INTERSTATE 39 FROM PORTAGE TO THE MARQUETTE-COLUMBIA COUNTY LINE.

*** 5 INJ *** BEGINNING OF TORNADO TRACK OF 8 MILES FROM 232 PM TO 250 PM CDT. TRACK ENDED 3 MILES SOUTH OF CAMBRIA. BROAD AREA OF DAMAGE ABOUT 2 MILES WIDE AT TIMES WITH ESTIMATED SPEEDS OF 60 TO 90 MILES. HOWEVER...SEVERAL EMBEDDED VORTICES WITH DAMAGE OF 100 TO 120 MPH. THOUSANDS OF TREES SNAPPED OR UPROOTED...A COUPLE DOZEN POWER POLES SNAPPED...AT LEAST 4 BARNS DESTROYED...AND ROOF DAMAGE TO SEVERAL HOMES. A GRAIN SILO WAS SHEARED IN HALF. SEE PUBLIC INFORMATION STATEMENT. HP SUPERCELL EVIDENT.

*** 1 INJ *** EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT REPORTED ONE INJURY ASSOCIATED WITH SOME VARIETY OF THUNDERSTORM DAMAGE NORTH OF RIO AT A CAMPGROUND. IT IS UNKNOWN WHETHER OR NOT THIS IS RELATED TO A TORNADO AT THIS TIME.

A ROOF OFF A HOME ON DUNNING ROAD. ALSO MULTIPLE REPORTS OF STRUCTURAL DAMAGE IN THE PORTAGE TO PARDEEVILLE AREAS...STRETCHING NORTHWEST TO THE WISCONSIN DELLS. IT IS UNKNOWN IF ALL OF THESE REPORTS ARE RELATED TO A TORNADO...BUT DISPATCH INDICATED THEY HAD MULTIPLE REPORTS OF TOUCHDOWNS IN COLUMBIA COUNTY. TIME IS ESTIMATED.
PEA SIZED HAIL ALSO REPORTED.

*** 4 INJ *** DELAYED REPORT. EMERGENCY MANAGER REPORTS TORNADIC DAMAGE NEAR COUNTY HIGHWAY G IN THE CAMBRIA/RANDOLPH AREAS OF FAR NORTHEAST COLUMBIA COUNTY. A HOUSE HAD ITS WINDOWS BLOWN OUT...A SHED WAS DESTROYED...A SILO WAS BADLY DAMAGED. 4 MINOR INJURIES WERE REPORTED. THE TORNADO ALSO SPUN A POLICE CAR AROUND. THEY SAID THERE IS A CLEAR PATH OF DAMAGE.
WATER OVER AT LEAST SEVERAL STREETS IN THE CITY. NO DEPTH GIVEN.

DELAYED REPORT FROM EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT. EF-1 RATING. MOVED FROM 3.4 S RANDOLPH IN EXTREME EASTERN COLUMBIA COUNTY TO 5.6 NW SOUTH BEAVER DAM IN DODGE COUNTY. LASTED 6 MINUTES FROM 308 PM TO 314 PM. THE PATH LENGTH WAS ABOUT 3.1 MILES AND PATH WIDTH WAS AROUND 75 YARDS. PEAK WINDS WERE PROBABLY AROUND 90 TO 95 MPH. A BARN WAS MOSTLY DESTROYED DUE TO WINDS THAT BLEW IN A SIDE DOOR...CAUSING THE WALLS TO FAIL. THE FLYING DEBRIS THEN SHEARED OFF A SMALL PART OF THE TOP OF A SILO.

IT IS UNKNOWN WHETHER OR NOT THE DAMAGE WAS CAUSED BY A BRIEF TORNADO OR EXTREME STRAIGHT LINE WIND DAMAGE. A ROOF WAS RIPPED OFF A HOME AND CARS WERE THROWN. FURTHER INVESTIGATION WILL BE NEEDED TO DETERMINE WHETHER OR NOT THIS WAS A TORNADO. THIS OCCURRED ON THE SOUTHWEST SIDE OF STOUGHTON.

DUE TO HEAVY RAINFALL OVER THE WEEKEND...LAKE DELTON HAD AN EXCESS OF WATER...AND BEGAN CUTTING A NEW CHANNEL TO THE WISCONSIN RIVER AT ABOUT 746 AM. THE CHANNEL HAS SINCE WIDENED TO 300 FEET WIDE. TREES AND POWER LINES HAVE BEEN SWEPT DOWNSTREAM...AND 3 HOMES HAVE ALSO BEEN LOST. SINCE THE CHANNEL HAS BEEN CUT...LAKE DELTON HAS DROPPED ABOUT 6 FEET. MOST CITY ROADS UNDER WATER. A RIVER THAT PASSES UNDER PARK AVENUE ON THE SOUTH SIDE OF TOWN IS OVER ITS BANKS.
ALMOST 75 PERCENT OF THE ROADS IN RIPON ARE CLOSED DUE TO FLOODING. 3 TO 4 FEET OF STANDING WATER IS COMMON OVER THE SOUTHERN HALF OF THE CITY. 4.2 INCHES OF RAIN HAS FALLEN IN THE PAST 4 HOURS.

INTERSTATE 90/94 BETWEEN MILE MARKERS 92 AND 106 IS CLOSED DUE TO FLOODING ISSUES. ALSO...HIGHWAY 33 IS CLOSED FROM THE INTERSTATE WEST TO THE SAUK COUNTY LINE. NUMEROUS ROADS ARE WASHED OUT COUNTY-WIDE.
FLASH FLOODING REPORTED IN MOST TOWNS AND CITIES ACROSS THE COUNTY. NO WATER DEPTH ESTIMATES WERE GIVEN...BUT LAW ENFORCEMENT SAID THAT THE FLOODING IS PRETTY MUCH A COUNTYWIDE PROBLEM.
